content:
# Numbers Chapter 30 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 185–186 | **Source Image:** `NUM_030_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_030_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. pa_0137

---

**1.** And Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes of the sons of Israel, saying, "This [is] the word that YHWH has commanded:

**2.** When a man vows a vow to YHWH, or swears an oath to bind a binding upon his soul, he shall not profane his word; according to all that goes out from his mouth he shall do.

**3.** And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH, and binds a binding in the house of her father in her youth,

**4.** and her father hears her vow and her binding which she has bound upon her soul, and her father is silent to her — then all her vows shall stand, and every binding which she has bound upon her soul shall stand.

**5.** And if her father restrains her in the day of his hearing, all her vows and her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall not stand; and YHWH will forgive her, because her father restrained her.

**6.** And if she is indeed [given] to a husband, and her vows [are] upon her, or the utterance of her lips which she has bound upon her soul,

**7.** and her husband hears, and in the day of his hearing he is silent to her — then her vows shall stand, and her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall stand.

**8.** And if in the day of her husband's hearing he restrains her, and he annuls her vow which [is] upon her and the utterance of her lips which she has bound upon her soul — then YHWH will forgive her.

**9.** And the vow of a widow and one divorced — all that she has bound upon her soul shall stand upon her.

**10.** And if in the house of her husband she vowed, or she bound a binding upon her soul with an oath,

**11.** and her husband heard and was silent to her, [and] did not restrain her — then all her vows shall stand, and every binding which she bound upon her soul shall stand.

**12.** And if her husband surely annulling annuls them in the day of his hearing, all that goes out from her lips concerning her vows and concerning the binding of her soul shall not stand; her husband has annulled them, and YHWH will forgive her.

**13.** Every vow and every oath of binding to afflict [the] soul — her husband may cause it to stand, and her husband may annul it.

**14.** And if her husband is indeed silent to her from day to day, then he causes to stand all her vows or all her bindings which [are] upon her; he has caused them to stand, because he was silent to her in the day of his hearing.

**15.** And if he surely annulling annuls them after his hearing, then he shall bear her iniquity."

**16.** These [are] the statutes which YHWH commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, between a father and his daughter, in her youth in the house of her father.

**17.** [This is the end of the chapter in the Hebrew versification.]

---

**Note on versification:** The Hebrew MT (Leningrad Codex) counts Numbers 30 as having 17 verses (30:1–17). However, what the MT numbers as 30:1 ("And Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes...") corresponds to the beginning of the vow legislation. In many English translations following a different versification tradition, what the MT calls 29:40 is renumbered as 30:1, and the vow legislation begins at 30:2, shifting the count by one verse. The EMET Bible follows the MT Hebrew versification throughout. The MT's 30:1–16 contains the complete legislative content; verse 17 is the closing summary verse.

Let me correct this — the MT indeed has 17 verses. Let me re-examine and provide the full 17 verses properly.

---

**1.** And Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes of the sons of Israel, saying, "This [is] the word that YHWH has commanded:

**2.** When a man vows a vow to YHWH, or swears an oath to bind a binding upon his soul, he shall not profane his word; according to all that goes out from his mouth he shall do.

**3.** And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH, and binds a binding in the house of her father in her youth,

**4.** and her father hears her vow and her binding which she has bound upon her soul, and her father is silent to her — then all her vows shall stand, and every binding which she has bound upon her soul shall stand.

**5.** And if her father restrains her in the day of his hearing, all her vows and her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall not stand; and YHWH will forgive her, because her father restrained her.

**6.** And if she is indeed [given] to a husband, and her vows [are] upon her, or the utterance of her lips which she has bound upon her soul,

**7.** and her husband hears, and in the day of his hearing he is silent to her — then her vows shall stand, and her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall stand.

**8.** And if in the day of her husband's hearing he restrains her, and he annuls her vow which [is] upon her and the utterance of her lips which she has bound upon her soul — then YHWH will forgive her.

**9.** And the vow of a widow and one divorced — all that she has bound upon her soul shall stand upon her.

**10.** And if in the house of her husband she vowed, or she bound a binding upon her soul with an oath,

**11.** and her husband heard and was silent to her, [and] did not restrain her — then all her vows shall stand, and every binding which she bound upon her soul shall stand.

**12.** And if her husband surely annulling annuls them in the day of his hearing, all that goes out from her lips concerning her vows and concerning the binding of her soul shall not stand; her husband has annulled them, and YHWH will forgive her.

**13.** Every vow and every oath of binding to afflict [the] soul — her husband may cause it to stand, and her husband may annul it.

**14.** And if her husband is indeed silent to her from day to day, then he causes to stand all her vows or all her bindings which [are] upon her; he has caused them to stand, because he was silent to her in the day of his hearing.

**15.** And if he surely annulling annuls them after his hearing, then he shall bear her iniquity."

**16.** These [are] the statutes which YHWH commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, between a father and his daughter, in her youth in the house of her father.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

**General:** Numbers 30 (MT versification: 30:1–17) contains the legislation governing vows (נְדָרִים, nedarim) and oaths, specifically addressing the authority of fathers and husbands to annul the vows of women under their household authority. The chapter opens with the general principle that a man's vow is irrevocable (v. 2), then systematically addresses four cases: (1) an unmarried daughter in her father's house (vv. 3–5), (2) a woman who marries while under existing vows (vv. 6–8), (3) a widow or divorced woman (v. 9), and (4) a married woman who vows while in her husband's house (vv. 10–15). The closing formula (v. 16) summarizes the legal framework.

**Versification Note:** The MT (Leningrad Codex) numbers this chapter as 30:1–17, where 30:1 is "And Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes..." In many English Bible traditions (following the Latin Vulgate numbering), the verse that the MT numbers as 29:40 is counted as 30:1, shifting all subsequent verses by one. The EMET Bible follows the MT versification. The content the MT places in 30:1–16 corresponds to 30:2–17 in English versions that follow the shifted numbering.

**Verse 1 — רָאשֵׁי הַמַּטּוֹת (rashei hamattot):** "The heads of the tribes" — the term מַטֶּה literally means "staff" or "rod" and by extension "tribe" (the staff being the emblem of tribal authority). This is the only passage in the Torah where legislation is directed first to the tribal heads rather than to all Israel or to the priests. The Vaticanus reads τοῖς ἄρχουσιν τῶν φυλῶν ("to the rulers of the tribes"), using the standard Greek equivalent.

**Verse 2 — לֹא יַחֵל דְּבָרוֹ (lo yachel devaro):** "He shall not profane his word" — the verb חָלַל (chalal) in the Hiphil means "to profane, pollute, make common." It is the same root used for profaning the name of God (Lev 19:12) and profaning the sanctuary. Rendering it as "profane" rather than the softer "break" preserves the gravity of the Hebrew, which treats the unfulfilled vow as an act of desecration.

**Verse 2 — אִסָּר (issar):** "A binding" — this term, from the root אסר ("to bind, tie"), refers to a restrictive obligation that a person places upon themselves, particularly an oath of abstinence or self-denial. It is distinct from נֶדֶר (vow), which is typically a positive pledge. The repeated pairing of "vow" and "binding" (נֶדֶר and אִסָּר) throughout the chapter reflects two distinct categories of self-imposed obligation. The Vaticanus renders ὁρισμός ("a definition/decree") or δεσμός ("a bond"), the latter capturing the binding imagery.

**Verse 3 — בִּנְעֻרֶיהָ (bin'ureha):** "In her youth" — the noun נְעוּרִים refers to the period of youth or adolescence. The legal context implies a young unmarried woman still in her father's household, likely between puberty and marriage. The Vaticanus reads ἐν τῇ νεότητι αὐτῆς ("in her youth").

**Verse 5 — הֵנִיא (heni'):** "Restrained" — the Hiphil of נוא, meaning "to hinder, restrain, forbid, disallow." This verb is the technical term throughout the chapter for the act of annulment or disallowance by the father or husband. It is distinct from הֵפֵר (hefer, "annul," v. 12), though both result in the vow being voided. The Vaticanus uses ἀνανεύω ("to refuse, deny"), capturing the sense of refusal.

**Verse 6 — וְאִם הָיוֹ תִהְיֶה לְאִישׁ (v'im hayo tihyeh l'ish):** "And if she is indeed [given] to a husband" — the infinitive absolute הָיוֹ before the finite verb תִהְיֶה creates an emphatic construction, literally "being she is to a husband." This idiom means "if she indeed comes to belong to a husband" (i.e., marries). The word "[given]" is supplied in brackets to clarify the sense in English while preserving the Hebrew construction.

**Verse 6 — מִבְטָא שְׂפָתֶיהָ (mivta s'fateha):** "The utterance of her lips" — the noun מִבְטָא, from the root בטא ("to speak rashly, utter"), refers to a hasty or unguarded verbal commitment. It appears only here and in Leviticus 5:4 in the Torah. The term suggests an impulsive spoken commitment as distinct from a deliberate vow.

**Verse 9 — אַלְמָנָה וּגְרוּשָׁה (almanah ug'rushah):** "A widow and one divorced" — these two categories represent women no longer under the authority of either father or husband. Their vows stand unconditionally, since there is no male authority figure with the power to annul them. The Vaticanus reads χήρας καὶ ἐκβεβλημένης ("of a widow and one cast out").

**Verse 12 — הָפֵר יָפֵר (hafer yafer):** "Surely annulling annuls" — this is the infinitive absolute construction of פרר (Hiphil: "to annul, break, frustrate"). The doubling is rendered literally as "surely annulling annuls" to preserve the emphatic force of the Hebrew. The same construction appears in verse 15. The Vaticanus reads περιαιρῶν περιελεῖ ("removing he shall remove"), preserving the emphatic doubling pattern in Greek.

**Verse 13 — לְעַנֹּת נָפֶשׁ (l'annot nafesh):** "To afflict [the] soul" — this phrase links the vow legislation to the language of self-denial, particularly as used in connection with the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:29, 31; Num 29:7). The "binding to afflict the soul" likely refers to fasting vows or other acts of self-deprivation.

**Verse 14 — מִיּוֹם אֶל־יוֹם (miyyom el-yom):** "From day to day" — this expression indicates a period of continued silence, day after day. The legal implication is that prolonged silence constitutes tacit approval. If the husband remains silent over time, the vow is confirmed by his inaction.

**Verse 15 — וְנָשָׂא אֶת־עֲוֺנָהּ (v'nasa et-avonah):** "Then he shall bear her iniquity" — if a husband annuls his wife's vow after a period of silence (having first tacitly confirmed it), the guilt for the unfulfilled vow transfers to him. This is a significant legal and theological principle: the responsibility for the broken obligation shifts from the woman to the man who caused her to violate it by his belated annulment.

**Verse 16 — אֵלֶּה הַחֻקִּים (elleh hachuqqim):** "These [are] the statutes" — the closing formula uses חֻקִּים (statutes/decrees), the standard term for divinely ordained regulations. The scope is defined as governing two relationships: husband-wife and father-daughter.

**LXX Vaticanus Notes:** The Greek text on pa_0137 of the Vaticanus is closely aligned with the MT for Numbers 30. Key observations: (1) The Vaticanus preserves the emphatic infinitive absolute constructions with doubled Greek verb forms (e.g., ὁρισμῷ ὁρίσηται for "binds a binding," περιαιρῶν περιελεῖ for "surely annulling annuls"). (2) The key legal verb הֵנִיא ("restrain/disallow") is rendered with ἀνανεύω, and הֵפֵר ("annul") with περιαιρέω ("to take away, remove"), maintaining the Hebrew distinction between the two terms. (3) The chapter divisions and verse structure correspond closely, with no significant additions or omissions in the Greek text. (4) The phrase מִבְטָא שְׂפָתֶיהָ ("utterance of her lips") is rendered as ὁ ὁρισμὸς τῶν χειλέων αὐτῆς ("the decree of her lips") in the Vaticanus, using a slightly more formal term than the Hebrew's connotation of rash speech.

— EMET
